---
output: github_document
---

<!-- README.md is generated from README.Rmd. Please edit that file. -->

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse   = TRUE,
  comment    = "#>",
  fig.path   = "man/figures/README-",
  out.width  = "100%",
  fig.retina = 2,
  dpi        = 144
)
set.seed(1L)
```

# multisiteDGP <img src="man/figures/logo.png" align="right" height="139" alt="" />

<!-- badges: start -->
[![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-yellow.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)
<!-- badges: end -->

**Data-generating processes for multisite trial simulations**

You have a multisite trial to design, a meta-analysis to plan, or an
estimator you want to stress-test. You need a defensible scenario you
can take to your manuscript reviewer — one with a citable provenance,
realistic site-effect heterogeneity, plausible per-site sampling
errors, and the option to inject the dependence between effects and
precisions that real trials exhibit.

`'multisiteDGP'` is the toolkit. Rather than requiring researchers to
assemble latent effects, sampling-error margins, and dependence
structures from raw variance components, `'multisiteDGP'` allows
specification through intuitive quantities:

- **Site counts and per-site sample sizes**: "How many sites, and how
  large is each?"
- **Heterogeneity ratio**: "How much do site effects vary relative to
  within-site sampling error?"
- **Defensible presets**: "What does a JEBS-, Walters-, or Weiss-style
  scenario look like?"

These applied questions are translated into a four-layer simulation
pipeline — latent effects, site-size margins, precision dependence,
observed estimates — backed by a catalog of eight distribution shapes
for the latent site-effect distribution. Every default has a citation;
every layer has a contract; every diagnostic answers a verification
question.

## Installation

```r
# Install from GitHub
# install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("joonho112/multisiteDGP")

# Install from CRAN, when available
# install.packages("multisiteDGP")
```

## Quick start

```{r quick-start, eval = FALSE}
library(multisiteDGP)

# Verify a defensible scenario reproduces canonically.
# Scenario: 50-site education trial calibrated to the JEBS paper preset.
dat <- sim_multisite(preset_jebs_paper(), seed = 1L)

# Inspect the simulated dataset.
print(dat)
#> # A multisitedgp_data: 50 sites, paradigm = "site_size"
#> # Realized vs intended:
#> #   I: realized=0.250 (no target)
#> #   R: realized=7.583 (no target)
#> #   sigma_tau: target=0.200, realized=0.207, PASS
#> #   rho_S: target=0.000, realized=-0.193, PASS
#> #   Feasibility: WARN (n_eff=13.098)
#> # A tibble: 50 × 7
#>   site_index    z_j   tau_j tau_j_hat  se_j  se2_j   n_j
#>        <int>  <dbl>   <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> <int>
#> 1          1 -0.582 -0.116    0.652   0.426 0.182     22
#> 2          2 -0.619 -0.124   -0.315   0.577 0.333     12
#> 3          3 -1.11  -0.222   -0.633   0.256 0.0656    61
#> 4          4  1.36   0.273    0.357   0.426 0.182     22
#> 5          5 -0.405 -0.0809  -0.00723 0.280 0.0784    51
#> 6          6  0.884  0.177   -0.203   0.385 0.148     27
#> # ℹ 44 more rows

# Read the realized informativeness off the diagnostics attribute.
attr(dat, "diagnostics")$I_hat
#> [1] 0.2500832

# Visualize the latent and observed site effects.
plot_effects(dat)

# Stamp the scenario for reproducibility — the hash is identical
# every time you rerun the same call.
canonical_hash(dat)
#> [1] "c52e75f276d82836"
```

In a single call you have a citable scenario, a printed dataset, a
realized-target diagnostic, a publication-ready plot, and a stable
provenance hash.

## Key features

### 1. Intuitive specification

Specify designs through site counts, per-site sample sizes, and the
between-site heterogeneity (`sigma_tau`):

```r
# Using a preset (recommended for most users).
dat <- sim_multisite(preset_education_modest(), seed = 1L)

# Or specify the design directly.
dat <- sim_multisite(
  J = 50L, true_dist = "Gaussian",
  sigma_tau = 0.20, nj_mean = 50, cv = 0.50,
  seed = 1L
)
```

### 2. Catalog of eight distribution shapes

Move beyond Gaussian site effects to study estimator behavior under
realistic departures from normality:

```r
# Skew-normal latent effects with moderate right skew.
dat_skew <- sim_multisite(
  preset_education_modest(true_dist = "SkewN", theta_G = list(slant = 4)),
  seed = 1L
)
```

The eight shapes — Gaussian, Student-t, skew-normal, asymmetric
Laplace, two-component mixture, point-mass slab, user callback, and a
Dirichlet-process-mixture bridge — share a standardized-residual
convention so `sigma_tau` (between-site heterogeneity) means the same
thing across scenarios.

### 3. Sampling-error margins and dependence structure

Generate per-site standard errors from the design's site sizes, and
inject intentional dependence between site effects and precisions:

```r
# Negative correlation between effects and precisions, via Gaussian copula.
dat_dep <- sim_multisite(
  preset_education_modest(dependence = "copula", pearson_corr = -0.30),
  seed = 1L
)

attr(dat_dep, "diagnostics")$rho_P_residual
#> [1] -0.2429586
```

### 4. Diagnostics and audits

Verify a design behaves as intended before you commit to a simulation
run:

- **`attr(dat, "diagnostics")`** — realized heterogeneity ratio,
  effect-precision rank correlation, distribution-shape summaries.
- **`scenario_audit()`** — sweep over a design grid and flag scenarios
  where realized targets drift from declared ones.
- **`plot_effects()`, `plot_funnel()`, `plot_dependence()`** — three
  visual checks for the latent distribution, sampling-error mass, and
  effect-precision dependence.

### 5. Adapters for downstream analysis

Hand a simulated dataset directly to common multisite and
meta-analysis packages without reshaping by hand:

```r
dat <- sim_multisite(preset_jebs_paper(), seed = 1L)
mf  <- as_metafor(dat)                       # 'metafor' random-effects meta-analysis
bg  <- as_baggr(dat, include_truth = TRUE)   # 'baggr' Bayesian hierarchical models
mp  <- as_multisitepower(dat)                # 'multisitepower' power calculation
```

## When to use `multisiteDGP`

`'multisiteDGP'` is particularly valuable for:

- **Multisite randomized trials** where you need realistic site-level
  summary data to evaluate estimators (J = 20–200).
- **Meta-analysis methods development** with controlled departures
  from normality, heteroskedasticity, and effect-precision dependence.
- **Power and design simulation** where defensible per-site sample
  sizes and sampling-error margins matter.
- **Replication studies** that need a reproducible, hash-stamped
  scenario specification.

For *fitting* multisite or meta-analytic models, hand the simulated
data to `'metafor'`, `'baggr'`, or `'multisitepower'` via the adapters.
The original Stan-based fitting code lives in `'siteBayes2'`.

## Vignettes

The package documentation is organized in two tracks. (Vignettes ship
with the v0.2 release; URLs below resolve once the pkgdown site
deploys.)

### Applied Track

| Vignette | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| [A1 · Getting started](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/a1-getting-started.html) | Your first simulation in 5 minutes |
| [A2 · Choosing a preset](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/a2-choosing-a-preset.html) | Walk through the nine bundled presets |
| [A3 · Diagnostics in practice](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/a3-diagnostics-in-practice.html) | Verify your design before you simulate |
| [A4 · Covariates and dependence](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/a4-covariates-precision-dependence.html) | Inject effect-precision dependence |
| [A5 · Calibrating to real data](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/a5-calibrating-real-data.html) | Match your simulation to a target study |
| [A6 · Case study — multisite trial](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/a6-case-study-multisite.html) | An end-to-end education-trial example |
| [A7 · Case study — meta-analysis](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/a7-case-study-meta-analysis.html) | An end-to-end meta-analysis example |
| [A8 · Cookbook](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/a8-cookbook.html) | Nine end-to-end recipes |

### Methodological Track

| Vignette | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| [M1 · The two-stage DGP](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/m1-statistical-dgp.html) | Mathematical framing of the four layers |
| [M2 · G-distribution catalog](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/m2-g-distribution-catalog.html) | The eight built-in shapes, side by side |
| [M3 · Margin and SE models](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/m3-margin-se-models.html) | Site-size-driven and direct-precision paths |
| [M4 · Precision dependence theory](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/m4-precision-dependence-theory.html) | Rank, copula, and hybrid methods compared |
| [M5 · Custom G distributions](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/m5-custom-g-distributions.html) | Bring your own latent distribution |
| [M6 · Adapters and downstream](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/m6-adapters-downstream.html) | The adapter contract and round-trip invariants |
| [M7 · Reproducibility and provenance](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/m7-reproducibility-provenance.html) | Seeds, hashes, and provenance strings |
| [M8 · Migration from siteBayes2](https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/articles/m8-migration-from-siteBayes2.html) | Translate legacy scenario specs |

## Citation

If you use `'multisiteDGP'` in your research, please cite:

```bibtex
@Manual{multisiteDGP,
  title  = {{multisiteDGP}: Data-Generating Processes for Multisite Trial Simulations},
  author = {JoonHo Lee},
  year   = {2026},
  note   = {R package version 0.1.0},
  url    = {https://joonho112.github.io/multisiteDGP/}
}

@Article{lee2025improving,
  title   = {Improving the estimation of site-specific effects and their distribution in multisite trials},
  author  = {Lee, JoonHo and Che, Jonathan and Rabe-Hesketh, Sophia and Feller, Avi and Miratrix, Luke},
  journal = {Journal of Educational and Behavioral Statistics},
  volume  = {50},
  number  = {5},
  pages   = {731--764},
  year    = {2025},
  publisher = {SAGE Publications Sage CA: Los Angeles, CA},
  doi     = {10.3102/10769986241254286}
}
```

For the canonical software citation including the IES funding
acknowledgment, run `citation("multisiteDGP")` after install — that is
the single source of truth; the BibTeX block above is a static
illustrative copy that may lag the installed package's metadata.

## Related work

- **Lee et al. (2025)** — site-specific effect estimation and
  informative priors for multisite heterogeneity. The package's
  defaults reproduce the JEBS paper's reference design.
- **Walters (2024)** — empirical Bayes methods in labor economics,
  with a school value-added application using Boston charter and BPS
  schools (Angrist, Hull, Pathak, & Walters, 2017). The
  `preset_walters_2024()` preset uses the chapter's parameters
  (J = 46 schools, $\sigma_\tau \approx 0.197$, mean per-school
  sample size 240) as a defensible value-added scenario.
- **Weiss et al. (2017)** — empirical cross-site variation benchmarks
  from 16 education and training multisite randomized trials. The
  `preset_education_*` family uses these ranges for site counts,
  mean site sizes, and cross-site effect standard deviations.
- **`'siteBayes2'`** — the companion fitting package. Use
  `'multisiteDGP'` to *generate* defensible scenarios; use
  `'siteBayes2'` to *fit* the Stan models.

## Support

This research was supported by the Institute of Education Sciences,
U.S. Department of Education, through Grant R305D240078 to the
University of Alabama. The opinions expressed are those of the
authors and do not represent views of the Institute or the U.S.
Department of Education.

<https://ies.ed.gov/use-work/awards/improving-estimation-site-specific-effects-and-their-distribution-multisite-trials-practical-tools>

### References

* Lee, J., Che, J., Rabe-Hesketh, S., Feller, A., & Miratrix, L.
  (2025). Improving the estimation of site-specific effects and their
  distribution in multisite trials. *Journal of Educational and
  Behavioral Statistics*, 50(5), 731–764.
  doi:10.3102/10769986241254286
* Walters, C. R. (2024). Empirical Bayes methods in labor economics.
  In C. Dustmann & T. Lemieux (Eds.), *Handbook of Labor Economics*
  (Vol. 5, pp. 183–260). Elsevier.
  doi:10.1016/bs.heslab.2024.11.001
* Weiss, M. J., Bloom, H. S., Verbitsky-Savitz, N., Gupta, H.,
  Vigil, A. E., & Cullinan, D. N. (2017). How much do the effects
  of education and training programs vary across sites? Evidence
  from past multisite randomized trials. *Journal of Research on
  Educational Effectiveness*, 10(4), 843–876.
  doi:10.1080/19345747.2017.1300719

## License

MIT © [JoonHo Lee](https://github.com/joonho112). See `LICENSE`.
